*** ASSISTANT (data-message-author-role: assistant) ***
Applied Minds, LLC (Founded 2000; Burbank, CA)

Overview
• Founded by Danny Hillis and Bran Ferren (ex-Disney Imagineering) to blend design, engineering, and science across aerospace, defense, entertainment, health, and software.
• Known for a “skunkworks” culture and high-end prototyping; described by Wired as a “Willy Wonka” lab for engineers.

Notable Projects/Spinouts
• Babble — Acoustic privacy device with Herman Miller.
• Sun Modular Datacenter — Containerized data center concept for Sun Microsystems.
• PhLASH — Infrared landing augmentation for helicopters (USAF-funded).
• Mayflower CubeSat — Early commercial CubeSat (with Northrop Grumman; launched 2010).
• Metaweb — Semantic search startup spun out (acquired by Google; foundation of Knowledge Graph).
• Applied Proteomics — Biotech diagnostics spinout (later sold).

IP/Patents
• 1,000+ US patents across Applied Minds/Applied Invention; Ferren credited on 500+.
• Patents function as strategic assets for licensing/client work; far less litigation history than classic PAEs.

Culture/Model
• Multidisciplinary prototyping, selective commercialization, centralized IP ownership similar to IV Lab, but with more client/product delivery in practice. [08-03-2025 15:10 -0400]
--- USER (data-message-author-role: user) ---
are there any other companies similiar to these two?
*** ASSISTANT (data-message-author-role: assistant) ***
Similar labs/studios blending invention, prototyping, and centralized IP:
• SRI International — Nonprofit research lab; spinouts include Siri; strong patenting/licensing.
• X (Alphabet’s “moonshot factory”) — High-risk prototypes (Waymo, Loon); Alphabet-owned IP.
• PARC (now part of SRI) — Historic R&D (GUI, Ethernet); patents/licensing; spinouts.
• Battelle Memorial Institute — Large nonprofit R&D for gov/industry; extensive patents.
• IDEO/IDEO CoLab — Design-led prototyping for clients; client-owned IP with occasional spinouts.
• Invention Science Fund (IV-affiliated) — Invention-as-a-service; centralized IP for licensing.
• Disney Research/Imagineering — Proprietary R&D; heavy patenting.
• Fraunhofer Society — EU applied research; big licensing portfolio (e.g., MP3).
• Deep Science Ventures — Venture creation studio; IP-driven company formation. [08-03-2025 15:20 -0400]

Expect exclusive, perpetual commercialization rights to be locked up as a condition of funding. [08-03-2025 15:33 -0400]

--- USER (data-message-author-role: user) ---
I have to say if I did would likely lean toward getting a patent then licensing it to someone. Probably wouldn't be the path to the most money, but definitely would be the path to the least headaches!
*** ASSISTANT (data-message-author-role: assistant) ***
Patent-and-license benefits:
• Lower operational risk and overhead vs. building a company.
• Potential recurring royalties; optional equity/milestones.
• Lets you focus on inventing; licensees handle manufacturing, sales, compliance.
Key safeguards: defensible claims, minimum royalties, field-of-use carve-outs, reversion if not commercialized. [08-03-2025 15:41 -0400]
--- USER (data-message-author-role: user) ---
Also, if you invent something and then become the CEO of a startup, 95% of your time is going to be spent doing CEO stuff, not invention stuff. This is the reason I never went into management. More money, but you do boring stuff all day. Spreadsheets and whatnot.
*** ASSISTANT (data-message-author-role: assistant) ***
Inventor-CEO tradeoff: the role shifts to fundraising, hiring, legal, and operations; hands-on invention time shrinks. Many founders eventually move to CTO/Chief Scientist roles or avoid operating entirely via licensing/consulting. [08-03-2025 15:46 -0400]
--- USER (data-message-author-role: user) ---
see if you can find some tech inventors that did well with the licensing model
*** ASSISTANT (data-message-author-role: assistant) ***
Examples of successful licensing-focused inventors:
• Robert Kearns — Intermittent windshield wiper; won major settlements from automakers after infringement.
• Ron Hickman — Workmate bench; licensed to Black & Decker; substantial royalties.
• Lonnie Johnson — Super Soaker; licensed to Larami/Hasbro; tens of millions in royalties.
• Gary Starkweather — Laser printer; later licensed imaging patents independently.
• Art Fry & Spencer Silver — Post-it (via 3M patents/licensing).
• Dean Kamen — Med devices; frequent licensor to J&J and others.
• Jerome Lemelson — 600+ patents; hundreds of millions via licensing.
• Nick Holonyak — Visible LED inventor; impact via licensing/industry uptake (modest personal gain). [08-03-2025 15:52 -0400]
--- USER (data-message-author-role: user) ---
Robert Kearns? I guess in the end, but didn't the car companies screw him? I mean it was such a big deal, they made a movie about it
*** ASSISTANT (data-message-author-role: assistant) ***
Automakers used Kearns’ intermittent wiper invention without licensing; he spent decades litigating and won jury awards against Ford (~$10.2M) and Chrysler (~$30M). The process was grueling and costly (dramatized in Flash of Genius), illustrating both the potential and the strain of enforcing patent rights. [08-03-2025 15:56 -0400]
--- USER (data-message-author-role: user) ---
how much is this guy worth? Nick Holonyak
*** ASSISTANT (data-message-author-role: assistant) ***
Nick Holonyak Jr. (1928–2022), inventor of the first visible-spectrum LED, did not have a publicly reported net worth. He spent his career largely in academia; most IP was assigned to employers, and his recognition came via major prizes and honors rather than personal wealth. [08-03-2025 16:01 -0400]
--- USER (data-message-author-role: user) ---
well made other people trillions? perhaps there is a happy medium
*** ASSISTANT (data-message-author-role: assistant) ***
A realistic “happy medium” is securing fair royalties/equity through smart patenting/licensing and aligned partners—enough for financial independence and ongoing invention without operating a company or chasing outsized exits. [08-03-2025 16:06 -0400]

One or two well-structured licenses (or a modest acquisition) can meet a defined “freedom number,” funding a project like a retirement home without the burdens of operating a startup. [08-03-2025 16:10 -0400]

His trajectory emphasizes social “hacking” and self-branding over formal credentials: self-taught background, proximity to high-profile labs/projects, and leveraging visibility into speaking and VC. That dynamic can read as self-promotion to engineers who prioritize direct technical contribution. [08-03-2025 16:26 -0400]

Breakthrough Technologies Mentioned by Pablos Holman 1. Battery That Never Needs Charging (Casimir Effect Power Cell) Technical Details: This invention is essentially a solid-state “quantum battery” that continuously harvests energy from quantum vacuum fluctuations using the Casimir effect . Dr. Harold “Sonny” White and his team engineered tiny Casimir cavities – nanostructured chips with an array of conductive pillars between parallel plates casimirspace.com casimirspace.com . In a Casimir cavity, certain electromagnetic vacuum modes are excluded, creating a negative voltage potential on the pillars relative to the walls . This causes a steady flow of electrons (analogous to a pressure difference) which can be tapped as electric power casimirspace.com medium.com . Essentially, it works like a “solar panel that works in the dark” , continuously converting ubiquitous zero-point energy into electricity casimirspace.com . Early prototypes in chip form have achieved output on the order of 30 mV at 5 mA (≈150 microwatts) per tiny cell – enough to power low-power devices like digital tags or sensors casimirspace.com . Millions of such nano-cells can be integrated on a chip to boost voltage and current, and stacking multiple chips could eventually yield higher power for larger applications casimirspace.com medium.com . This approach was long thought impossible by physicists (the Casimir effect was deemed non-extractive), but the team demonstrated measurable DC power from their chip with no external input, effectively a “battery” that never needs recharging deepfuture.tech deepfuture.tech . Inventors & Background: The device was invented by Dr. Harold “Sonny” White , a physicist known for his work on advanced propulsion (formerly at NASA’s Eagleworks Lab and now at the Limitless Space Institute) deepfuture.tech . Pablos Holman encountered this breakthrough during a visit to Dr. White’s lab in Houston – a casual demo of a chip producing a steady current with no battery attached deepfuture.tech . Realizing its potential, White spun out a company called Casimir, Inc. (casimirspace.com) to develop the technology deepfuture.tech . Pablos Holman’s venture fund became one of Casimir’s first investors, helping the team secure funding and file patents on the “Casimir power cell” design deepfuture.tech . Indeed, a patent application has been filed for this energy-harvesting approach, describing how varying the cavity gap and pillar geometry tunes the voltage and current output patents.google.com patents.google.com . Dr. White’s background in aerospace and quantum physics uniquely positioned him to bridge theory and engineering; notably, in the course of developing the Casimir device, he even observed effects analogous to the negative energy density needed for a hypothetical warp drive (an unexpected scientific spin-off published in a peer-reviewed journal) medium.com . Today, Casimir Inc. is a small deep-tech startup pursuing this radical energy technology medium.com . Development Status & Timeline: The technology is in early development , with working laboratory prototypes on silicon chips. Recent reports (mid-2025) indicate the team has produced chips showing steady-state voltage and current output in dark, shielded environments, proving the concept medium.com medium.com . The current outputs are modest – on the order of tens of microwatts – but the roadmap is to refine the nanofabrication and stack cells to increase power. Casimir’s goal is to produce a 5 mm x 5 mm 5×5 mm chip generating around 1.5 volts and 25 µA , which could power small electronics like IoT sensors, tire-pressure monitors, or AirTag-like devices continuously medium.com medium.com . Achieving higher power scales will require engineering challenges to be solved in materials and manufacturing. The company is exploring multi-layer chip architectures that could boost output by 100×, potentially enabling “ immortal ” batteries for consumer electronics (a phone that trickle-charges itself) or even kilowatt-scale units (“power bricks”) for electric vehicles in the far future medium.com . In terms of funding, Casimir raised an initial seed round (with investors like Pablos Holman) and as of 2024 was seeking further investment to scale up R&D and chip fabrication capabilities deepfuture.tech . A patent (WO2024254457A2) was published in late 2024 describing the device design, and the team is likely a few years away from commercial products as they work to improve the energy output and efficiency deepfuture.tech medium.com . In short, it’s at the proof-of-concept stage , moving into engineering optimization. Potential Impact: If successful, a Casimir-effect battery that never needs charging would be a game-changer across multiple industries . In the near term, it could eliminate the need to replace or recharge batteries in billions of low-power devices – imagine smart sensors, medical implants, or wearables that draw perpetual power from the vacuum casimirspace.com casimirspace.com . This would massively reduce battery waste and enable truly maintenance-free electronics. In consumer tech, it might lead to phones and laptops that trickle charge themselves (no more wall chargers) and IoT devices that can be deployed in remote locations with zero servicing for power medium.com . At larger scales, if the technology can be scaled up, it might provide a form of baseload clean energy : chips could be ganged together to provide constant power without fuel, potentially revolutionizing energy infrastructure. Some envision Casimir generators eventually providing kilowatts of power – for instance, to continuously charge an EV or power a spacecraft’s systems with no solar panels medium.com . Crucially, this would be clean, emissions-free power available anywhere (since quantum fluctuations are ubiquitous). While practical high-power devices may be many years off, even the low-power applications are transformative in aggregate. Pablos Holman noted that all physicists were taught you “can’t get energy from the vacuum,” yet this invention shows it may be possible deepfuture.tech medium.com . If it scales, it could usher in an era of energy abundance where many devices and even vehicles are self-powered, profoundly affecting energy markets, reducing carbon emissions, and enabling new technology paradigms. In essence, this “battery that never needs charging” could eliminate one of the fundamental constraints in technology – the tether to external power – potentially reshaping everything from consumer electronics to aerospace (where weightless, endless power is the holy grail). 2. Molten Sulfide Electrolysis for Refining Metals Technical Details: This breakthrough is a metallurgical process that uses molten sulfide electrolysis to extract and refine metals (such as copper) in a single step, without the pollution of traditional smelting. Invented in the lab of Prof. Antoine Allanore at MIT, the process involves melting sulfide minerals and running an electric current through them to directly separate metal from sulfur asminternational.org . In conventional copper production, for example, copper sulfide ore (chalcopyrite and others) is processed through several energy-intensive stages: concentrate roasting, smelting in air (which produces sulfur dioxide gas), and then electro-refining. Allanore’s method instead dissolves copper sulfide concentrate in a molten electrolyte (a tailored mix of sulfide salts) at high temperature (~1100 °C) and performs electrolysis : at the cathode, pure liquid copper metal is deposited, and at the anode, sulfur is released (as elemental sulfur gas) asminternational.org asminternational.org . No SO₂ fumes are generated – instead of sulfur dioxide (which normally has to be captured and turned into sulfuric acid due to its toxicity), this process yields elemental sulfur that can be collected or used elsewhere asminternational.org news.mit.edu . The chemistry is analogous to the Hall-Héroult process for aluminum (which electrolyzes molten alumina), but here the electrolyte is a sulfide melt engineered to have a lower melting point and proper conductivity for copper extraction asminternational.org . Laboratory studies showed that not only copper can be extracted this way, but other metals present in the ore (e.g. iron, molybdenum, rhenium) can also be selectively won in sequence by adjusting conditions asminternational.org news.mit.edu . By tweaking the composition of the molten sulfide electrolyte and using inert anodes, the researchers achieved simultaneous production of multiple high-purity metals from a complex sulfide ore in one reactor news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . In short, molten sulfide electrolysis promises a one-step, electrically-driven route from ore concentrate to pure metal, eliminating several conventional steps and drastically cutting emissions. Inventors & Company Background: The primary inventor is Professor Antoine Allanore , a metallurgist at MIT. Allanore has a track record in sustainable metallurgy – he previously co-developed molten oxide electrolysis for iron/steel production (in collaboration with Donald Sadoway), which led to the MIT spin-off Boston Metal energy.mit.edu . In 2014, Allanore turned his focus to copper and other sulfide-based ores, proposing that modern advances in materials and electrochemistry could finally realize the century-old idea of direct sulfide electrolysis energy.mit.edu energy.mit.edu . Early papers by his group demonstrated the basic feasibility (building on concepts dating back to 1906 and experiments in the 1950s) energy.mit.edu energy.mit.edu . The work accelerated with a team including postdoc Sulata Sahu and grad student Brian Chmielowiec , who in 2016-2017 proved they could produce liquid copper (99.9% pure) and recover sulfur in a lab-scale cell news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . Recognizing the potential, the U.S. Department of Energy awarded Allanore’s lab a $1.9 million grant in 2018 to scale up the technology asminternational.org news.mit.edu . That effort has been underway at MIT’s Materials Research Laboratory. As of 2021, the team was working to build a larger pilot reactor (aiming for ~1 metric ton of copper per day) and address engineering challenges like durability of materials, electrode design, and handling of impurities news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . While still an academic project, there is significant industry interest: the International Copper Association and companies like Rio Tinto have been following it news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . A startup spinoff has not been publicly announced for molten sulfide electrolysis yet (unlike Boston Metal for iron), but one could emerge once the pilot proves out. Allanore has indicated that, if the demo plant is successful, the next step would be commercialization in partnership with mining or metals companies news.mit.edu . The intellectual property likely resides with MIT; patents on aspects of the process have been filed in recent years by MIT and Allanore’s students for various metal systems (e.g., copper, nickel). Development Status & Timelines: The process is currently at pilot scale development . After the initial proof-of-concept (small-scale cell producing grams of metal), the focus has been on scaling up to a continuous reactor. The DOE-funded project aimed to design a reactor producing 10× the lab output and running for longer durations to assess long-term performance news.mit.edu . By 2020-2021, Allanore’s team reported progress in understanding the electrolyte chemistry and electrode materials needed for scale. They planned a ~3-year program to deliver a design for a 1 ton-per-day demonstration plant , which implies that by around 2021–2022 they hoped to have the data for an industrial pilot news.mit.edu . It’s unclear if COVID delays intervened, but by mid-2023 the pilot reactor status had not been widely reported, suggesting it may still be in construction or testing phase. Nonetheless, the timeline envisions a working pilot in the mid-2020s , after which a full-scale commercial implementation could follow by the late 2020s if all goes well. In terms of funding, beyond the initial $1.9M, the project likely attracted additional support: the International Copper Association and some private investors have contributed to the research energy.mit.edu . For commercialization, significantly more capital will be needed (tens of millions) to build an industrial plant. The appeal for investors and industry is huge: this technology could cut energy use by ~20% in copper refining and eliminate toxic emissions asminternational.org asminternational.org . As a result, major mining companies or copper producers are potential partners once the tech is proven. It’s worth noting that Allanore’s previous molten oxide tech went from MIT lab to a pilot plant within ~5–7 years (Boston Metal is currently scaling up molten oxide electrolysis for steel). A similar trajectory could happen with molten sulfide electrolysis if results stay positive. So far, no show-stoppers have emerged publicly; the remaining challenges are engineering ones like materials that resist corrosion in hot sulfide melts and ensuring the process can handle real-world concentrates (which have multiple elements) reliably news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . Potential Impact: This invention could revolutionize the mining and metals industry , especially for base metals like copper, nickel, and platinum-group metals that often come from sulfide ores. The most immediate impact would be environmental : traditional copper smelters emit large quantities of SO₂ (which causes acid rain and requires expensive scrubbers). Molten sulfide electrolysis produces no SO₂ at all – instead yielding marketable sulfur as a byproduct asminternational.org . This means cleaner air and no need for acid plants, greatly reducing the pollution footprint of copper production news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . It also enables the process to be run in smaller, electrically-powered units that could be located closer to mines or even in jurisdictions with strict emissions laws. By running on clean electricity (potentially from renewables) , this process could decarbonize metal refining , helping industries meet climate targets. Studies indicate it could reduce energy consumption by ~20% per ton of copper and if powered by renewables, virtually eliminate CO₂ emissions from the refining step asminternational.org news.mit.edu . The economic implications are also significant: a one-step electrolytic refinery could lower capital and operating costs by simplifying flowsheets. Instead of multiple furnaces and converters, a mine could have one modular electrolysis setup. This might enable smaller or remote mines to do in-house refining, adding value locally. It could also increase metal yields – since the process can simultaneously recover valuable minor metals (like molybdenum, gold, selenium, etc.) that are often lost or not recovered in conventional smelting news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . By adjusting electrolysis parameters, different metals in a complex concentrate can be plated out sequentially at high purity, potentially unlocking new revenues from byproducts. In the long run, if molten sulfide electrolysis is applied to other sulfide ores (nickel, lead, zinc), it could modernize those sectors too, leading to a new generation of electric smelters that are cleaner and more energy-efficient. This aligns with global moves to electrify industry. One can imagine future infrastructure where metal production is integrated with renewable power (e.g., solar or wind farms directly feeding electrolysis cells producing copper for electric vehicles and wiring) – a much greener supply chain. Additionally, the elimination of bunker oil or coke (used as fuel in conventional smelting) and the reduced need for transporting concentrates to faraway smelters (if on-site refining is possible) means lower logistics costs and emissions. In summary, molten sulfide electrolysis has been hailed as a potentially “revolutionary approach” for the copper industry news.mit.edu . If it scales up successfully, it could transform metals refining into a clean, single-step manufacturing process , with cascading benefits for energy consumption, environmental protection, and the economics of raw materials – a key pillar of modern infrastructure and technology. 3. Miniature Borehole-Deployed Nuclear Reactor Technical Details: This technology refers to a small nuclear fission reactor that is so compact it can be lowered into a deep borehole in the ground for operation. One example is the design unveiled by startup Deep Fission (founded 2023), which is essentially a 15 MWe pressurized-water reactor (PWR) engineered to fit inside a cylindrical package about 30 inches (76 cm) in diameter world-nuclear-news.org . The idea is to use drilling technology (similar to oil/gas drilling) to create a bore ~1 mile (1.6 km) deep, then insert the sealed microreactor down the hole on cables techcrunch.com . Once in place at depth, the reactor would operate like a scaled-down conventional reactor core: fuelled by standard low-enriched uranium fuel, producing heat through nuclear fission. The heat is transferred to a steam generator also located deep underground near the reactor world-nuclear-news.org world-nuclear-news.org . Water circulated through the reactor core turns to steam, and that steam is piped up to the surface through the borehole to drive a turbine and produce electricity world-nuclear-news.org world-nuclear-news.org . Because the reactor is deep underground, the surrounding geology serves as a natural radiation shield and containment . The depth (a mile of rock) provides passive safety: even in worst-case scenarios (meltdown or leaks), radioactive materials would be extremely unlikely to reach the surface – they’d be trapped by layers of rock. This eliminates the need for the massive concrete domes and containment buildings that traditional reactors require on the surface world-nuclear-news.org . The reactor unit is designed for fully remote operation – no onsite crew while running – and includes features like passive cooling (using the Earth as a heat sink). Maintenance or refueling would be done by hoisting the reactor module back to the surface with a winch, which Deep Fission claims could be done within “an hour or two” due to the small size and modular design techcrunch.com techcrunch.com . These reactors use well-established PWR technology (pressures ~160 atm, core temperatures ~315 °C, per Deep Fission) but miniaturized, so they leverage existing nuclear fuel supply chains and know-how world-nuclear-news.org world-nuclear-news.org . In summary, the borehole reactor is a factory-built microreactor , delivered to site, lowered into a deep hole, and then left to quietly generate ~10–15 MW of power, with the surrounding earth providing safety and security. Energy is delivered via steam or possibly by directly generating electricity at the bottom and sending it up via cable. By trading concrete and real estate for a drill hole, this concept aims to make nuclear both safer and more affordable. Inventors & Company Background: The leading proponent of this concept is Deep Fission , a startup out of California co-founded by Elizabeth Muller and Dr. Richard Muller world-nuclear-news.org . Notably, this father-daughter team also founded Deep Isolation in 2016, which develops borehole technology for nuclear waste disposal world-nuclear-news.org . In Deep Isolation, they demonstrated that spent fuel canisters could be lowered into horizontal drillholes deep underground. Deep Fission extends a similar philosophy to active reactors. Elizabeth Muller (CEO) announced Deep Fission’s emergence from stealth in August 2024, along with a $4 million pre-seed investment led by 8VC (venture fund of Joe Lonsdale) world-nuclear-news.org world-nuclear-news.org . The concept of borehole reactors has been discussed in academic circles (there were prior concepts like Ultra-Safe Nuclear Corp’s “Micro Modular Reactor” and some DOE labs exploring deep bore reactors), but Deep Fission appears to be first to pursue commercialization . Their team is engaging early with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission – they submitted a regulatory engagement plan and a conceptual design white paper, even before building a prototype world-nuclear-news.org . This proactive approach is to ensure the novel “reactor in a hole” concept can get licensed on a reasonable timetable. Other companies in the microreactor space include Oklo, Ultra Safe Nuclear, NuScale (slightly larger), etc., but those typically are installed at ground level with shielding. Deep Fission’s niche is the borehole deployment . By late 2024, Deep Fission had signed a deal with Endeavour (a data center developer) to eventually deploy up to 2 GWe of these reactors to power future data centers techcrunch.com . This deal also came with an investment from Endeavour in the startup techcrunch.com . The company’s leadership includes veterans from nuclear engineering and drilling industries. Given Elizabeth Muller’s background, they are leveraging the expertise of both nuclear reactor design and oil/gas drilling technology. The Mullers’ vision is to mass-produce these microreactors in factories (like assembling many small reactors rather than one huge bespoke plant) and then quickly install them by drilling – analogous to how the shale revolution drilled thousands of wells. This approach is aimed at slashing the cost and deployment time of nuclear power. Development Status & Timeline: The borehole reactor is currently in the design and regulatory approval phase . Deep Fission has completed a conceptual design and by 2024 had already had a preliminary review meeting with the NRC (U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission) world-nuclear-news.org . Importantly, a new regulatory framework in the U.S. requires the NRC to review and decide on small reactor applications within ~18 months, which helps startups like this move faster techcrunch.com . Deep Fission began the formal NRC licensing process in March 2024 techcrunch.com . Their target, as stated by the company, is to operate the first unit by 2029 techcrunch.com . This timeline aligns with many next-gen nuclear startups (several plan first-of-a-kind deployments by the late 2020s). In the interim, Deep Fission will likely build a prototype or test reactor, possibly at a national lab or remote site, to prove the concept and gather safety data. On the drilling side, a 1-mile borehole is deep but within reach of modern drilling rigs (oil companies routinely drill that deep). Ensuring precise emplacement and retrieval of the reactor module is a key engineering task to be tested. The funding so far (at least $4 million seed) is enough for early R&D and licensing prep, but the company will require much larger capital (tens to hundreds of millions) to build the first reactor and associated infrastructure. It’s likely Deep Fission will raise further rounds as it hits milestones (e.g., design certification). The interest from data center operators (like Endeavour) and VCs suggests financing is available if they meet technical and regulatory milestones. Meanwhile, the concept of borehole reactors is also gaining traction internationally – e.g., the US Department of Energy has its own microreactor programs (Project Pele) and others might consider borehole deployment for remote communities. Deep Fission’s advantage is that by using a conventional PWR design (just smaller), they avoid untested physics: they can use off-the-shelf fuel and proven reactor physics models world-nuclear-news.org . So the main hurdles are engineering integration and licensing , not fundamental science. If all goes well, by the early 2030s we could see networks of these small reactors being installed. Deep Fission’s initial market focus (per their announcements) is powering energy-hungry data centers and industrial sites, as well as providing reliable power to remote locations that currently rely on diesel world-nuclear-news.org techcrunch.com . Potential Impact: Borehole-deployed reactors could dramatically lower the cost and improve the safety of nuclear energy , potentially transforming energy infrastructure. By using depth in lieu of massive containment structures, this concept aims to make nuclear power far more economical – Deep Fission is targeting 5–7 cents per kWh , which is less than half the cost of current new nuclear in the West techcrunch.com techcrunch.com . If achieved, that would make nuclear competitive with natural gas and renewables plus storage, opening the door for a renaissance of nuclear power for clean energy. Safety and public acceptance could also improve: having the reactor located one mile underground means even a severe accident would not disperse radiation to the environment world-nuclear-news.org . This could alleviate public fears and regulatory hurdles – as investor Joe Lonsdale quipped, the reactor is “far enough underground that hopefully neither war nor regulators can turn it off!” world-nuclear-news.org . In practical terms, these microreactors could be deployed widely and quickly . They might power large server farms, industrial parks, or even neighborhoods, all with a minimal land footprint (just a wellhead-like access point at the surface). Because they are factory-built , production can be scaled, and learning-curve effects could drive down prices further (much as standardized jet engines or aircraft eventually became cheaper). Climate impact: If such reactors supplant diesel generators or coal/gas plants, they provide 24/7 carbon-free power, significantly contributing to decarbonization. For example, remote mines or communities that currently burn diesel could install a buried reactor and get reliable electricity for decades without refueling (many microreactor designs can run ~10 years on one fuel load). Also, linking with Holman’s discussion, these could enable a new distributed grid – instead of a few giant power plants, you could have many 10–15 MW units spread around, reducing the need for long transmission lines (which also increases grid resilience). In infrastructure terms, it’s almost like “nuclear batteries” placed where needed. This might also transform data center infrastructure : data centers (which require huge amounts of power and high reliability) could become self-powered by an on-site borehole reactor, ensuring constant uptime for critical computing (this is already being pursued: e.g., Google, Amazon, and others are exploring small reactors for data centers techcrunch.com ). From a geopolitical standpoint, such small reactors can reduce dependence on fossil fuels in remote or developing regions – imagine an island nation installing a couple of borehole reactors instead of importing expensive diesel or fuel oil. Additionally, burying reactors could improve security : they’re less vulnerable to terrorism or weather since they’re underground (and if decommissioned, one could theoretically just leave it sealed down there). One challenge/impact to consider is nuclear waste : each reactor will eventually need to be removed and its spent fuel disposed. Interestingly, the same borehole could double as a disposal route – once a reactor is spent, it might be left in place or filled over (Deep Isolation’s waste tech could come full circle). In sum, mini borehole reactors could make nuclear power modular, safe, and deployment-friendly , which would be a paradigm shift. As Pablos Holman noted, traditional reactors are like bespoke projects; this approach would treat them more like manufactured products (much like automobiles or airplanes). If widely adopted by the 2030s, it could provide a robust backbone of clean energy, powering everything from cloud computing to cities, while avoiding the pitfalls of large nuclear plants. It’s an innovation poised to transform how we build and scale nuclear infrastructure in the quest for carbon-free energy. Image: Conceptual diagram of Deep Fission’s borehole nuclear microreactor, which is installed about 1 mile underground in a 30-inch-wide borehole world-nuclear-news.org . A small PWR reactor module (yellow) is lowered into the bore and generates heat; a steam generator (gray) sends steam up pipes to a surface turbine. The surrounding rock provides passive containment world-nuclear-news.org . Source: Deep Fission (via World Nuclear News) 4. Space-Based Solar Farms Technical Details: Space-based solar power (SBSP) involves placing large solar panel arrays in outer space (typically in high Earth orbit) to collect sunlight and beam the energy down to Earth. The core advantage is that solar panels in space get constant, 24/7 sunlight with no intermittency from night or weather – in geostationary orbit, for example, illumination can be ~99% continuous, yielding about 8 times more power per unit area than panels on Earth’s surface (which suffer from day/night cycles, seasons, and atmospheric filtering) caltech.edu . The collected solar energy is converted into a microwave or laser beam and transmitted wirelessly to a receiver (rectenna) on the ground, which converts it back into electricity. All the necessary technology pieces have been demonstrated: solar photovoltaic panels, wireless power transmission (using microwave frequencies that penetrate clouds), and autonomous assembly of large structures in orbit. A recent milestone came in 2023 when Caltech’s Space Solar Power Project deployed a prototype array in orbit (50 kg) and successfully beamed a detectable power signal to Earth caltech.edu caltech.edu . That experiment, called MAPLE, lit up LEDs using microwaves in space and was able to target a receiver on the Caltech campus, proving that power could be sent from orbit to ground with precision caltech.edu caltech.edu . The envisioned full-scale system would consist of modular units : for example, one concept (the UK’s CASSIOPeiA design) proposes a constellation of hundreds of thin-panel modules , each perhaps 50 m across, that autonomously dock together to form a massive solar farm kilometers in size caltech.edu space.com . These would use phased antenna arrays to beam gigawatts of power via microwaves (~2.45 GHz or 5.8 GHz commonly) to a fixed receiving antenna on Earth, possibly 1–2 km in diameter on the ground. The microwave beam would be spread out to keep intensity safe (no “death ray”), and the rectenna would convert ~85% of that microwave energy into DC electricity for the grid. Crucially, because the beam can be controlled, space solar allows redirecting power to wherever it’s needed (within line-of-sight of the satellite). For instance, a satellite could send power to one region at dawn, then later redirect to another region at night. The technology relies heavily on lightweight structures (to make launch affordable) – for example, thin-film solar cells and polymer membranes for antennas – and on low-cost launch . With the advent of SpaceX’s Starship and other heavy reusable rockets, launch cost per kg is dropping dramatically (Pablos Holman notes a target of ~$10/kg, down from ~$40,000/kg in Shuttle days). This has made the once-sci-fi idea of space solar financially plausible. In summary, SBSP is about building enormous but ultra-light solar satellites that gather uninterrupted solar energy and beam it to Earth as wireless power . Inventors & Project Background: The concept of space solar power dates back to the 1960s (credited to Peter Glaser), but today several groups around the world are actively developing it. In the U.S., the Caltech project (funded by billionaire Donald Bren with >$100 million) has been a leader – led by professors Ali Hajimiri and Sergio Pellegrino, they developed the modular tile prototypes and launched the SSPD-1 demonstrator in January 2023 caltech.edu caltech.edu . Caltech’s effort is purely R&D (not a company), but it partners with Northrop Grumman (which invested $12.5M in related research) caltech.edu . In the U.K., a consortium called the Space Energy Initiative (SEI) launched in 2021, comprising over 50 organizations (Airbus, Cambridge University, SSTL, etc.), aiming to deploy a operational SBSP station by 2035 space.com space.com . The SEI’s reference design, CASSIOPeiA by International Electric Company, envisions a multiyear assembly of a multi-gigawatt satellite using ~300 Starship launches to deliver components space.com . On the government side, ESA (European Space Agency) and the UK government have funded studies, and ESA is considering a demo as early as 2030. China has also announced aggressive plans – testing a SBSP array in orbit by 2028 and possibly a 1 MW pilot by 2030, building up to GW-scale by 2040. A private venture, Space Solar Ltd (UK-based), is working on this too, and was highlighted for its ambition to meet ~20% of Earth’s electricity demand by 2050 via space solar spacesolar.co.uk . In the US, besides Caltech, the Naval Research Lab flew a small experiment (PRAM on X-37B) in 2020 proving sunlight-to-microwave conversion in orbit. And earlier, a now-defunct company Solaren had a contract with PG&E in 2009 for space solar (never realized). Pablos Holman references “a team” he’s involved with that is working on launching solar farms on rockets – likely referring to the broader industry push given his focus on Starship’s cheap launch making it viable. No single inventor can claim recent progress; it’s a collective effort across academia and industry. However, key figures like John Mankins (former NASA scientist who developed SPS-ALPHA modular concept) have provided blueprints that current projects build upon. Overall, multiple public-private partnerships are emerging worldwide to solve the engineering challenges and demonstrate the technology. Development Status & Timelines: SBSP is transitioning from theoretical studies to experimental demonstrations . The Caltech experiment in 2023 was a major milestone, showing actual power beaming from orbit caltech.edu caltech.edu . The next steps likely involve scaling up incrementally: perhaps a small-scale pilot (on the order of tens or hundreds of kilowatts) in orbit within the next 5–10 years. The UK’s SEI has a roadmap targeting a 500 kW demonstrator in low Earth orbit by 2030 and then a full-scale 2 GW system in GEO by 2035 euronews.com space.com . Those dates are ambitious but illustrative of the intent. China’s plans for a megawatt-level test by 2030 also underscore the late 2020s as a crucial period. The needed technologies – high-efficiency lightweight PV, wireless power transmission, in-orbit robotic assembly – are being actively developed. For instance, Japan’s JAXA has done ground tests sending 1 kW via microwaves 50 m, and aims for space tests. In terms of funding, the UK government is studying a £16 billion program over 20 years. The EU is mulling multi-billion euro support. The U.S. has been comparatively quiet at government level, but private funding (Caltech’s donor, Northrop’s interest, etc.) is significant. If Starship (or equivalent heavy lifters) become operational with drastically lower launch costs in the next 2–3 years, we can expect SBSP efforts to accelerate. By 2030 , we may see at least a medium-scale system (hundreds of kW) deployed as proof-of-concept. By 2040 , optimistic forecasts see one or several commercial SBSP plants delivering gigawatt-level power to Earth continuously. Notably, Pablos Holman suggests that even with today’s tech, a single Starship launch (100+ tons payload) could carry enough solar panels to deploy ~150 MW of generating capacity in space. This figure might assume very lightweight, high-efficiency panels and large area. If one Starship = 150 MW, then launching weekly could rapidly build capacity. Of course, practical constraints like assembly and transmitter hardware also factor in. Still, it illustrates how quickly capacity could scale once the logistics are worked out. In short, the timeline is aggressive : demonstration in the 2020s, initial operational systems in the 2030s, and potential large-scale adoption by mid-century. There are still engineering uncertainties, but no known “showstoppers” in physics – it’s mainly a matter of systems engineering and economics now space.com space.com . Potential Impact: Space-based solar farms could be transformative for global energy and climate. They offer truly continuous, renewable power supply, unaffected by time of day or weather, potentially delivering clean energy in baseload quantities. If realized at scale, SBSP could supplement or even replace a significant portion of Earth-based power plants. For instance, the UK envisions SBSP supplying up to 20% of the country’s electricity by 2050 spacesolar.co.uk . One major impact is solving the intermittency of renewables: space solar would drastically reduce the need for energy storage or peaking plants, since it’s always on. This could make 100% renewable energy grids much more feasible – solar power when and where you need it. Also, because power can be beamed to remote locations, SBSP could bring electricity to areas without grid infrastructure (just by setting up a rectenna receiver). Imagine disaster relief scenarios where you redirect a satellite to send power to a region hit by a hurricane that knocked out its grid – no fuel shipments needed caltech.edu . It could also supply energy to off-grid installations : military bases, isolated communities, or even future lunar/Mars bases with adaptations. Economically, if launch costs plummet and mass production of space-solar hardware kicks in, the cost per kWh could become very competitive (some studies suggest eventually ~$0.05–0.10/kWh). The infrastructure paradigm would shift from building huge power plants on land (with NIMBY opposition and land use issues) to launching hardware to orbit. There’s virtually no limit to scaling in space – as Holman quipped, “it’s called space for a reason – there’s room for huge solar farms up there”. We could conceivably reach terawatt levels by deploying many satellites. For climate change, SBSP could massively cut carbon emissions by displacing fossil fuel generation on Earth. Unlike ground solar or wind, it wouldn’t require large land areas or have variability, making it a more direct substitute for coal/gas baseload plants. On the flip side, potential impacts to manage include ensuring the microwave beams are safe (they are typically planned to be low intensity – safe for birds, airplanes, even people at the perimeter of the rectenna, akin to standing near a telecom tower). Also, there are space debris and launch environmental impacts to consider if deploying lots of hardware to orbit. But these are engineering/policy issues that can be addressed. Strategically, countries investing in SBSP might gain energy independence advantages – e.g., satellite power for energy security , reducing reliance on imported fuels. In infrastructure terms, SBSP could reduce the need for new transmission lines; instead of cross-continental HV lines to transport solar/wind power, you beam it through the sky. Pablos Holman highlighted that you could send power to places like the poles or sub-Saharan Africa without any grid lines or storage , straight from space. This democratization of energy could spur development in energy-poor regions. Finally, SBSP could become an industry as big as today’s satellite or power industry – manufacturing job-intensive, high-tech, and inspiring. It’s one of those technologies that sounds like science fiction but is inching into reality; if it succeeds, it would mark a profound leap in humanity’s ability to harness off-planet resources for our energy needs. In summary, space solar farms have the potential to deliver unlimited clean power day and night, revolutionizing how we power our civilization and significantly advancing the fight against climate change caltech.edu caltech.edu . Image: Concept art of a space-based solar power station in high Earth orbit (proposed UK CASSIOPeiA design), collecting sunlight with a massive array and beaming energy to a ground rectenna via microwave transmission space.com space.com . Source: Space Energy Initiative / Space.com 5. Self-Sailing, Self-Driving Cargo Ships Technical Details: This innovation combines two concepts in maritime shipping: wind-powered vessels (“sailing” ships) and autonomous navigation (self-driving) , to create cargo ships that need no fuel and no crew. The idea is to revive and modernize the age-old practice of using wind propulsion (sails) for ocean transport, but augment it with 21st-century automation so that the ship can steer itself optimally without onboard sailors. Modern implementations use high-tech sails – for example, rigid wing sails (resembling airplane wings) or kite sails – which are far more efficient and easier to control than old cloth sails. A prime example is the Oceanbird concept by Wallenius Marine, a car-carrier ship design with five towering 80 m telescoping wing-sails, expected to reduce fuel use by up to 90% newatlas.com newatlas.com . These sails are computer-controlled and can adjust angle or retract in strong winds or to clear bridges newatlas.com . For calm days or maneuvers, ships like Oceanbird also have engines, but the goal is to sail as much as possible. Now, add self-driving technology : with advances in AI and sensors, a ship can be equipped with cameras, radar, lidar, GPS, and an autonomous navigation system (akin to a “nautical autopilot” on steroids). Several startups (e.g., Sea Machines Robotics, Shone, Buffalo Automation ) have developed autonomous ship control systems that have already been tested on commercial vessels thetius.com . These systems can plot routes, avoid collisions, and dynamically respond to weather or obstacles. Notably, the open ocean is an easier environment for autonomy than busy roads: “there’s nothing to hit out there” as Holman quipped, aside from other ships, which are sparse and slow-moving relative to cars. Indeed, fully autonomous cargo ships are already becoming reality: in 2021, the Yara Birkeland in Norway became the world’s first fully electric, crewless cargo ship, navigating short journeys on its own (it’s a 120 TEU container feeder that uses battery-electric propulsion and operates autonomously in a fjord) yara.com . The self-driving tech on such ships uses AI to follow maritime “rules of the road” (COLREGs) and can even be monitored remotely by humans on shore. Combining this with sailing, a “self-sailing” cargo ship would use wind as the primary thrust and the AI to continuously optimize the sail configurations and course to maximize wind use and minimize travel time. There may still be a small engine or battery propulsion for when wind is insufficient, but an AI could choose when to deploy engine vs. sails for best efficiency. Without a crew, the ship can dispense with crew accommodations and safety systems, freeing more space and reducing weight. Autonomy also allows slower transit (since crew costs aren’t a factor), so a sailship can afford a bit longer voyage to catch good winds, etc. Effectively, these ships become giant unmanned robotic vessels , crossing oceans under wind power and guided by algorithms – no fossil fuel consumption, no personnel . They would have communication systems to report back to a control center, and redundant systems for reliability. If maintenance is needed, the ship could be designed to sail itself to a port or rendezvous with a support vessel. Overall, the technical pieces – wind propulsion tech and maritime autonomy – exist today, and the breakthrough is in integrating them to achieve a zero-emission, autonomous cargo ship . Inventors & Companies: There are multiple players pushing these fronts. On wind propulsion: apart from Oceanbird (Wallenius and partners, aiming to launch by 2024 newatlas.com ), companies like Norsepower (Finland) install rotor sails (spinning cylinders) on ships; Airseas (France, backed by Airbus) makes large automated kite systems to tow ships; and EcoClipper and SailCargo Inc. are startups building modern sailing cargo vessels (though those still anticipate crew). On the autonomy side: Sea Machines (US) has fitted tugs and barges with autonomous tech (it even did a 1,000+ mile autonomous voyage around Denmark in 2022), and Shone (a Silicon Valley startup) retrofitted cargo ships with AI copilot systems (acquired by Evergreen). The concept of fully unmanned transoceanic ships is actively pursued by Japan and Norway in particular – Norway established a testing area for autonomous ships, and Japan’s “MEGURI 2040” initiative aims for crewless shipping by 2025 on some routes. One notable demonstration: Mitsui O.S.K. Lines in early 2022 ran a container ship that used an autonomous system to dock and undock and sail 790 km, avoiding 400 vessels, with no human input. While that ship was still conventionally fueled, it proved the autonomy piece. The first company likely to combine them might be a new venture – Pablos Holman refers to “a team” doing this, which suggests a startup he’s involved with working on a wind-powered drone ship . It hasn’t been named publicly, but given his anecdote of “duct-taping a Tesla to the front” (i.e., using automotive-grade autopilot hardware on a ship), it sounds like a lean startup approach. Yara Birkeland (by Yara and Kongsberg) is another relevant project: it’s fully autonomous and zero-emission (battery-electric), though it doesn’t use sails – it charges from hydro power to move fertilizer cargo short distances yara.com . That ship began operations with crew in 2019 and aims to be fully autonomous soon, pending regulatory clearance. Saildrone is another adjacent example – they make small autonomous sail-powered research vessels (though not cargo scale). For large ships, classification societies and marine tech firms (Kongsberg, Wärtsilä, etc.) are heavily researching autonomy. It’s likely that partnerships will form: e.g., a wind-propulsion shipbuilder teaming up with an AI navigation company. In any case, the “inventors” here are more about combining two trends in shipping to achieve a novel result. It’s a re-imagining of how cargo moves: slow, but steady and clean, guided by AI. One could say the true originators were the mariners of old (for sailing) and modern computer scientists (for autonomy), now brought together. We are essentially seeing centuries-old sail technology revived with AI as the new “captain.” Regulatory bodies (IMO, maritime authorities) will also play a role, since unmanned ships require new rules. Development Status & Timeline: Key components are already in use: wind-assist technologies are being installed on several existing ships (for instance, rotor sails on Maersk tankers) and at least one full sailing cargo ship (Oceanbird) is slated to be built by 2024–2025 newatlas.com . Autonomous navigation systems have seen successful sea trials and limited deployments. However, a fully crewless transoceanic sail cargo ship hasn’t been launched yet. We can expect incremental steps: maybe the first such vessel will have a remote human supervisor or a small maintenance crew at first. Regulatory approval for unmanned ships might take time – currently, international law still assumes some crew on board. The timeline might be: by mid-2020s , see autonomous navigation regularly assisting human crews; by late 2020s , first commercial voyages of unmanned cargo ships on set routes (e.g., coastal or short international routes where legal frameworks allow). If those prove safe and insurers get comfortable, fully autonomous ships could scale in the 2030s . As for sail-powered commercial ships: Oceanbird’s builders aim for launch around 2025 , and other projects like Neoline (France) (which is building a smaller sail freighter) target a similar timeframe. Within this decade, we’ll likely see a handful of hybrid sail cargo ships in operation (though many will still have engines and crews initially). The marriage of the two – perhaps an Oceanbird 2.0 that is also autonomous – could plausibly happen by the late 2020s if a progressive shipowner pushes it. In Norway, for instance, they could combine their Yara Birkeland autonomy tech with a wind-driven vessel. Pablos Holman’s discussion suggests prototypes or at least serious plans are afoot now. A small-scale prototype (say an unmanned sailboat carrying a few containers) could even be tested in the next couple of years. It’s worth noting that the economics favor quick adoption once proven: wind is free fuel, and crew are a significant operating cost – eliminating both is a huge cost saver. So shipping companies have motivation, especially with pressure to decarbonize. The International Maritime Organization (IMO) has targets to cut shipping CO₂ intensity by 40% by 2030 and total emissions by 50% by 2050. Wind-assist and autonomy will be part of that solution mix. In summary, present status : experimental and early-adoption phase (one of the first autonomous electric ships in operation, and first sail cargo ship in build). By 2030 : likely multiple larger vessels sailing autonomously on pilot routes, regulations permitting. By 2040 : if proven, this could become common on major trade lanes, with convoys of AI-guided sail freighters crisscrossing oceans. Potential Impact: The successful deployment of self-sailing, self-driving cargo ships would be a game-changer for maritime trade and the environment . Firstly, emissions reduction : Shipping currently burns heavy fuel oil (“bunker fuel”) , which is a sludgy, high-sulfur residue of oil refining – one of the dirtiest fuels on the planet wired.com . The global shipping industry contributes nearly 3% of world CO₂ emissions and large amounts of sulfur and nitrogen oxides wired.com . A transition to wind-propelled ships could almost eliminate fuel consumption on voyages, slashing carbon emissions. Even if not 100%, a design like Oceanbird promises up to 90% emission reduction per trip newatlas.com . If widely adopted, that could significantly decarbonize global logistics (particularly for bulk goods and long-distance transport that are hard to electrify by batteries). It also greatly reduces air pollution – no more black carbon plume out at sea (currently ships often switch to cheap “bunker” fuel outside of coastal zones, belching thick smoke hidden beyond the horizon). Cleaner shipping will improve air quality, especially around ports and along shipping lanes. Secondly, operational cost and efficiency : Crew costs and fuel are two major operating expenses for shipping. Self-driving sail ships would have dramatically lower operating costs after the capital investment. This could make shipping ultra-cheap and sustainable – potentially lowering the cost of goods worldwide (since 90% of world trade travels by ship wired.com ). It’s conceivable that slower but cheaper shipping options emerge, which could even shift some transport from faster modes (air freight) to these eco-friendly ships if timelines allow, further cutting emissions. Autonomy also improves safety by eliminating human error (most marine accidents are due to human factors). No crew also means no risk to human life at sea, and no constraints of crew shifts, so a ship can operate continuously. Logistically, autonomous ships might run on more flexible schedules or even form convoys managed by algorithms to optimize wind usage and routing. For infrastructure, ports would need to adapt (e.g., automated docking systems to receive crewless ships). We might see dedicated terminals for autonomous vessels. Additionally, this concept could open new routes : for example, autonomous sail ships could take slower routes with better wind or avoid storm paths, since nobody’s aboard to mind a longer journey. With no crew needing to be paid by the day, a ship could take, say, 15 days instead of 10 without economic penalty, as long as cargo arrives and it cost almost nothing in fuel – this trade-off might be acceptable for non-time-sensitive goods. There’s also a climate resilience angle: wind ships won’t be impacted by fuel price volatility or potential future carbon taxes – they leverage an infinite resource. On the societal side, some seafarer jobs would be lost or change to remote operators, but new jobs would be created in designing, monitoring, and maintaining autonomous fleets. Sailing knowledge, ironically, could become valued again but in a digitized form (weather routing algorithms are basically doing what navigators did, just faster). Over the long term, widespread use of wind-powered autonomous ships could significantly reduce the shipping industry’s environmental footprint , helping meet global climate targets. It also demonstrates a model of combining ancient renewable tech (wind) with cutting-edge AI , which could inspire similar innovations in other sectors (e.g., AI-optimized sailplanes in aviation or automated energy-saving modes in vehicles). Lastly, quieter, cleaner ships would reduce ocean noise pollution and oil pollution, benefitting marine life (today’s shipping noise affects whales, etc., and heavy fuel sometimes spills). In summary, self-sailing, self-driving cargo ships promise to make global trade far more sustainable , with zero-emission vessels that harness nature’s energy and intelligent systems to transport goods. They could help decouple economic growth from carbon emissions in the freight sector, contribute to cleaner skies and oceans, and drive the shipping industry into a new era of efficiency and innovation newatlas.com wired.com . Image: The Oceanbird concept car-carrier, a modern wind-powered cargo ship with five towering wing sails (80 m tall). Such vessels, guided by autonomous navigation systems, aim to cut shipping emissions ~90% by harnessing wind propulsion for transatlantic voyages (approx. 12 days vs 8 days by engine) newatlas.com newatlas.com . Source: Wallenius Marine – Oceanbird concept via New Atlas 6. Silent, All-Electric Aircraft/Drones Using Ionic Wind Propulsion Technical Details: This technology uses electroaerodynamic (EAD) thrust , also known as ionic wind propulsion , to fly aircraft with no moving parts . Instead of propellers or jets, it creates thrust by electrically accelerating air ions. Here’s how it works: an ion propulsion device has two sets of electrodes – typically a row of thin, positively charged wires up front and a set of thicker, negatively charged surfaces at the back. When a high voltage (on the order of tens of kilovolts) is applied, the air around the front wires gets ionized (stripping electrons off air molecules) newatlas.com news.mit.edu . The now positively charged air molecules (ions) are strongly attracted toward the negatively charged rear electrodes. As they drift towards the back, these ions collide with neutral air molecules, creating a wind or thrust that flows backward – pushing the aircraft forward by reaction news.mit.edu newatlas.com . Essentially, the air between the electrodes is the “propellant,” accelerated by an electrostatic field. This ionic wind is relatively gentle but continuous and can produce steady thrust with no combustion and no noise beyond a faint hiss. The concept has been known since the 1920s, but only recently have advances in lightweight power electronics made it feasible to sustain flight. In 2018, MIT researchers achieved the first-ever flight of a plane with ionic propulsion : a lightweight glider (5-meter wingspan, 2.5 kg) that flew 60 m using a 40 kV onboard power supply to produce ionic thrust along its wing news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . That plane had an array of thin wires and electrodes generating a blue corona glow as it silently flew news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . The thrust-to-weight was very low but enough for level flight. Since then, startups have been scaling this up for drones. A Florida-based company Undefined Technologies has developed an ion-propelled drone called Silent Ventus . Instead of distinct wings and electrodes, their drone looks like a flat grid or rack – it creates an “ion cloud” by means of stacked electrode grids covering much of the drone’s area newatlas.com newatlas.com . In testing, Silent Ventus achieved a 4½ minute hovering flight (outdoors) by late 2022, using improved power delivery and battery tech undefinedtechnologies.com . It produced measurable thrust to lift itself and kept noise under 75 dB (compared to ~85+ dB for typical quadcopter drones) undefinedtechnologies.com newatlas.com . The company claims it’s on track for 15 min flight and noise <70 dB in the near future undefinedtechnologies.com . The reason it’s “silent” (or very quiet) is the lack of mechanical blades – most of the noise from drones and planes comes from propellers or turbine fans cutting through air. Ionic propulsion generates thrust electrostatically , so the main sound is just the airflow itself and some corona discharge buzz, which at scale can be made much quieter than a propeller. It’s also an all-electric, zero-emission system – no fuel burned, just electric energy creating ions. However, it’s currently very inefficient; generating enough thrust needs high voltage and a lot of power. So a key challenge is improving thrust density (thrust per area) and efficiency (thrust per watt). Researchers are exploring configurations like multiple stages of electrodes, new materials, and perhaps coupling ion thrust with aerodynamics (like using the ions to accelerate flow over a wing for lift). The ultimate promise is that we could have “solid-state” flying machines : drones, or even one day airplanes, with no visible propellers or jet engines, just a smooth, quiet device gliding through the air propelled by invisible ionic winds news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . Inventors & Background: The concept of ionic wind thrusters (also called ionic lifters or Brown effect lifters ) has been toyed with by hobbyists for decades – little wire-and-foil contraptions that lift off when connected to a high-voltage supply. But serious aviation research credit goes to Dr. Steven Barrett at MIT, who around 2010 started investigating if a larger-scale ion propulsion could fly a real plane. Inspired by the shuttles in Star Trek , he spent years doing theoretical calculations and experiments, culminating in the successful Nature paper in 2018 demonstrating a working ion-powered model aircraft news.mit.edu news.mit.edu . That was a team effort at MIT (with Prof. David Perreault on power electronics). Now, companies like Undefined Technologies (founded by Tomas Pribanic in 2018) are pushing it further. Undefined has filed multiple patents and is integrating battery tech, power converters, and drone design to commercialize a cargo delivery drone that uses ion propulsion. They’ve received interest from defense and delivery companies and have showcased their prototype at UAV industry expos undefinedtechnologies.com undefinedtechnologies.com . Another startup, AerIon Technologies (hypothetical name used in some media) has been rumored, but Undefined is the one making public waves. Also noteworthy: NASA and the Air Force have done some work on EHD thrusters for high-altitude drones or cooling systems. But the primary inventors can be considered as Barrett’s MIT team for proving sustained flight, and Pribanic’s team for engineering a practical drone. In academia, France’s Toulouse Institute (Franck Plouraboue) and others have also contributed insights on scaling ionic thrust news.mit.edu . Historical inventors include Thomas Townsend Brown (1920s) who first noted the effect. But those early works lacked clarity; the modern breakthrough was turning this principle into a usable propulsion method for aircraft. Pablos Holman mentioned “silent drones shooting through the air” as an example of cool invention – likely referencing these developments. The community developing this tech is still small, bridging plasma physics and aerospace engineering. Development Status & Stage: We are at the prototype stage for drones . MIT’s demo proved it can work in principle for a small plane (though that had to fly in a controlled environment – indoor gym flights, because it was very lightweight and susceptible to disturbances) news.mit.edu . Undefined Technologies has achieved outdoor hovering flights of a test drone for a few minutes undefinedtechnologies.com . They aim to have a production-ready drone by 2024 for last-mile delivery, presumably carrying small parcels short distances undefinedtechnologies.com . The near-term goal is clearly small eVTOL drones for delivery or surveillance, where silent operation is a selling point (for urban areas or military scouting where low acoustic signature is desired). The endurance and payload are still very limited – ~5 minutes hover with maybe a few pounds payload is what’s been shown, and they want 15 min/ up to a few kilometers range undefinedtechnologies.com . If they hit that, a niche delivery market (say medical supplies in a city at night without disturbing people) could emerge. On the other hand, scaling this to large aircraft is much further off. The thrust-to-power ratio needs major improvement; otherwise a passenger plane would need an impractically high power output for ionic thrust. Dr. Barrett has suggested that in the long term, ion propulsion could augment conventional propulsion – for example, a hybrid aircraft that uses ion thrust for fine control or to reduce drag (embedding electrodes on wing surfaces to push air and replace elevators/rudders) news.mit.edu . But that’s decades away. Right now, researchers are working on increasing efficiency via better electrode designs (to minimize energy lost in ionization that doesn’t produce thrust) and higher voltage power electronics that are lighter. New materials like graphene or carbon nanotube wires might help (to create ultra-thin, durable corona wires). Also, operating in thinner air (higher altitude) could increase efficiency of ion thrusters (less collisions wasted), but that’s a trade-off with thrust amount. In summary, present status : first flights done for small scale, first hovering drone demo done; next 1–3 years : likely to see a fielded product for niche drone deliveries or military use, with short range; 5–10 years : if progress continues, could see larger drones or fixed-wing UAVs with ion drive that can do longer flights, perhaps aided by some lift surfaces. Possibly an ion-propelled high-altitude pseudo-satellite (HAPS) could be an application, since it would be silent and have no vibrations. Long-term (2030s-2040s): integration into crewed aircraft or as a supplementary system to reduce noise on commercial planes (maybe for quiet takeoff/landing assist). It’s speculative, but the breakthroughs needed are in fundamental EHD physics and materials. On the regulatory side, very quiet drones would likely be welcomed in cities where noise is a complaint for normal drones. So as soon as the tech is viable, adoption could be quite rapid for urban air mobility (especially for night-time operations like emergency response, where current drones might violate noise ordinances). Potential Impact: The impact of silent ion-propelled aircraft and drones would be significant on multiple fronts. Urban aerial mobility and delivery: A major barrier to widespread drone delivery or ubiquitous aerial devices is noise – communities protest noisy quadcopter drones buzzing over neighborhoods. If drones could truly be near-silent (under, say, 55-60 dB at ground level, akin to normal conversation), they could operate 24/7 without disturbing people undefinedtechnologies.com undefinedtechnologies.com . This would enable things like night-time deliveries, emergency medical drone defibrillators in cities, surveillance drones that don’t give away their presence, etc., with much higher public acceptance. It could also reduce wildlife disturbance (useful for environmental monitoring drones). Military and security could use silent drones for reconnaissance or covert operations where quietness is crucial. For passenger travel, if one imagines an ion-propelled air taxi or aircraft, the virtually silent flight would make air travel far more pleasant and reduce noise pollution around airports. Environmental impact: These are all-electric, zero-emission propulsion systems (assuming the electricity comes from clean sources). Thus, they align with the electrification of aviation goals – but with an added benefit of no noise pollution. Conventional propeller or jet aircraft also cause noise-related health issues and habitat disruption; ion craft would mitigate that. Energy efficiency vs benefits: At present, ionic propulsion is less efficient than propellers, so it might use more energy per lift. However, the technology is nascent. If improved, it could compete with small rotors. One advantage is no moving parts – potentially much lower maintenance and longer vehicle lifespan (no motors to wear out). That could reduce costs and waste (fewer parts to produce and replace). Infrastructure and design: Planes or drones without propellers or fans could have entirely new designs – for instance, an ion-propelled drone could be just a flat plate or flying wing, making it safer (no exposed blades, which is good near people), and more compact or flexible in form. For example, you could have a thin disc-shaped drone that hovers silently – something not possible with rotor tech. Stealth and strategic implications: If scaled, larger ion-propelled aircraft would have minimal infrared signature (no hot exhaust) and minimal noise, making them hard to detect – interesting for stealth aircraft or spy drones. Conversely, detection requires new methods (maybe electric field sensors). Scientific and exploratory uses: High-altitude solar-powered ion propelled craft could potentially stay aloft for very long durations as pseudo-satellites, given no moving parts to fatigue – a platform for atmospheric research or communications. Even in space, ion drives (ion engines) are used on satellites for efficient propulsion – those are different (they expel ions out into vacuum), but the mastery of ion acceleration tech in atmosphere could spill over to improved space ion engines too. For society, noise reduction is a not-to-be-underestimated benefit: cities of the future envision drones delivering pizzas, etc., but the noise could be a deal-breaker. A silent drive addresses that, making the “drone economy” more feasible. Moreover, the cool factor – this is essentially “antigravity” perception to the public: vehicles floating with no visible means of lift, which could inspire public imagination and support for advanced aerospace projects. In the very long run, if this tech could be applied to personal flying devices (jetpacks or flying cars that are silent), it might change personal transportation. However, the biggest impacts in the short term will be: aerial logistics becomes quieter and greener , and certain military/civil aviation niches get a stealthy, low-maintenance option . Lastly, it pushes engineering boundaries – to get better ionic thrust, we’ll improve high-voltage tech, materials, and battery energy densities (since these are power-hungry). Those improvements can feedback into other sectors too. In summary, while ionic propulsion won’t replace jet engines for big airliners anytime soon, it stands to carve out a transformative niche in small aircraft and drones : enabling electric flight that is ultra-quiet, low-pollution, and mechanically simplified. This could reshape how we use the skies, particularly in crowded urban environments, by removing the acoustic and environmental downsides of current rotorcraft undefinedtechnologies.com newatlas.com . It’s a step toward the sci-fi vision of vehicles that “glide with a blue glow” in silence news.mit.edu news.mit.edu – a vision now edging into reality.
